result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Attacker
    participant B as Responder B
    participant S as Server S

    I->>B: start responder with xB = B
    I->>B: encrypt((NY,B), pkB)
    Note over B: beginAparam(B,B)
    B->>S: (B,B)
    S-->>B: sign((pkB,B), skS)
    Note over B: beginAfull(B,B,pkB,pkB,NY,Nb)
    B-->>I: encrypt((NY,Nb,B), pkB)
    I->>B: encrypt(NY, pkB)
    Note over B: Check passes because code tests returned NY
    Note over B: endBparam(B,B) and endBfull(B,B,pkB,pkB,NY,Nb)
    B-->>I: sencrypt(secretBNa, NY)
    I->>I: sdecrypt(sencrypt(secretBNa,NY), NY) = secretBNa
result.explanation: The responder accepts a session completed only by the attacker. In the trace, the attacker starts B as responder, chooses NY, obtains B's own certificate from S, and answers the final challenge with encrypt(NY, pkB). This succeeds because the responder checks message 7 against NY instead of the fresh responder nonce Nb. As a result, endBparam/endBfull occur without any matching initiator beginBparam/beginBfull, and secretBNa is leaked since it is later protected with the attacker-chosen NY.
result.patch_suggestion: In processResponder, fix the final verification to require the responder's nonce Nb (and use the responder's actual secret key variable): replace `if nonce_to_bitstring(NY) = decrypt(m3, skB) then` with `if nonce_to_bitstring(Nb) = decrypt(m3, skxB) then`. This makes message 7 prove knowledge of Nb, which the attacker does not learn from the trace.
result.notes: The evidence directly shows failures for `secretBNa`, `endBparam`, and `endBfull`; the initiator-side queries remain true in this run.
